kept.text:
DevOps Engineering Guide

If it is version control → Git
If it is code collaboration → GitHub / GitLab
If it is CI/CD pipelines → GitHub Actions / Jenkins
If it is containerization → Docker
If it is orchestration → Kubernetes
If it is infrastructure as code → Terraform
If it is configuration management → Ansible
If it is cloud platforms → AWS / Azure / GCP
If it is monitoring → Prometheus / Grafana
If it is logging → ELK Stack
If it is alerting → Alertmanager
If it is secrets management → Vault
If it is service discovery → Consul
If it is load balancing → Nginx / HAProxy
If it is artifact management → Nexus / Artifactory
If it is release automation → ArgoCD
If it is scalability → Auto Scaling Groups
If it is reliability → SRE practices
If it is security → DevSecOps (shift-left security)
If it is fast delivery → automate → test → deploy → monitor

Grab the DevOps ebook: https://t.co/fFkAVQgVJX

kept.text:
Man no wonder y’all are burnt out and going insane. Any time you come into any amount of friction with a thing you just bail and go to the next and repeat. 

Had a post go viral venting frustration about GitHub and you idiots got mad at me for not switching immediately to gitlab. 

Had a post got semi-viral venting frustration about Claude code and you idiots got mad at me because I won’t bail and switch immediately to codex. 

Maybe if you stopped spinning your fucking wheels you’d get shit done.

Hit a merge conflict? Don't switch branches.

@​coderabbitai fix merge conflict

kept.text:
I thought this morning that maybe the market is mispricing GitLab. For context, their stock has fallen in value by >50% over the last year.

So I spent some time this morning testing out the product end-to-end (creating a repo, checking out trending projects, pushing code, opening a merge request and reviewing, etc.).

Well, there is a reason not everyone is talking about migrating to them during this otherwise obvious moment of opportunity: the product is just not that different than GitHub, and in a lot of ways it's IMVHO just worse.

Unless they reinvent the product in a big way, GitLab doesn't seem to be well positioned to take advantage of GitHub's current moment of weakness. Their products are just too similar to each other.

Ultimately, I decided the company is not worth buying at this moment in time. Maybe that'll change, but right now the source control products I'm paying attention to don't resemble GitHub at all; whatever comes next will look completely different.

kept.text:
my grandpa died of a spinal tumor. rare cancer. 

limited treatment options. doctors couldn't do much bec the science wasn't there yet.

this is why bio x ai excites me.

it takes >12 years and $2.6B to bring ONE drug to market. 90% of candidates fail in trials

for rare cancers like spinal tumors, the economics barely justify the research

ai is compressing that. 

@gitlab's founder @sytses went founder mode on his own stage 4 spinal cancer, fed 25TB of his data to ai, found the treatment doctors missed. now relapse free.

the diseases that used to be "too rare to invest in" might finally get their shot 

that's what this intersection means to me.

kept.text:
What they don’t tell you when you start with CI/CD:
it’s not hard…
it just feels like it’s judging you.

Pipelines fail. YAML breaks. Nothing explains why.

So yeah, most of us avoid it. 👀👇
